config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I do not think society should automatically take priority over the rights of the individual, because that can easily become an excuse for abuse and oppression. In my view, a healthy society protects individual rights first, while still setting limits when one person's freedom clearly harms others. From my perspective, the best balance is not "society over the individual," but a society strong enough to protect both the common good and personal dignity.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The rights of the individual should not be treated as secondary to society by default. From my perspective in the United States, that idea is dangerous because governments and majorities can always claim "the common good" to justify censorship, discrimination, or abuse if individual rights are not firmly protected. Society does matter, but its legitimate role is to set fair limits when one person's actions directly harm others, not to override basic rights whenever it is convenient. A free society works best when individual rights are the foundation, not the obstacle.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not automatically take priority over the rights of the individual, because in practice that often becomes a shortcut for silencing minorities and justifying state overreach. From my perspective in Indonesia, I understand the appeal of harmony and collective responsibility, but I have also seen how easily “for the public good” can be used to pressure people into obedience instead of fairness. Society does matter, but its interests should be balanced against individual rights, with clear limits so the majority cannot trample personal dignity. A good society is not one that sacrifices the individual first, but one that protects people well enough that the common good is built on justice, not coercion.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over the rights of the individual by default, because that is exactly how majorities and governments end up excusing censorship, discrimination, and abuse. From my perspective in the United States, I value the idea that rights are protections against the crowd, not privileges society can suspend whenever it wants a cleaner or more convenient outcome. That said, individual rights are not absolute in every situation; society can impose fair limits when someone is directly harming others, but those limits need to be narrow and justified. To me, the burden should always be on society to prove why a right must be restricted, not on the individual to prove why their freedom deserves to exist.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should never be treated as a blank check to override individual rights, especially because that logic is exactly what can be abused by authorities and majorities. From my perspective in Indonesia, I understand why people value harmony and social order, but I do not accept the idea that those values should come before basic dignity, freedom, and fairness. Society is strongest when it protects individuals well, not when it teaches them to surrender their rights for the sake of convenience or control. Limits can be justified when someone clearly harms others, but the default should always be that individual rights come first.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From my perspective in the United States, that principle is too easy to abuse because “society” often ends up meaning whoever has power at the moment, whether that is a government, a majority, or a vocal moral crowd. I do not buy the idea that harmony or convenience justifies weakening basic rights; the burden should stay on society to prove a restriction is truly necessary and narrowly tailored to prevent real harm. A healthy society protects the common good by defending individual dignity, not by treating individual rights as something to sacrifice first.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
